nativeRange.habitat: Subterranean limestone cave pools and streams (cave morph); surface rivers, streams, and ponds (surface morph). Cave populations occupy the Cueva Chica and surrounding cave systems of the Sierra de El Abra region, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas, Mexico. Surface populations range from the Rio Grande drainage in Texas south through much of Mexico.

decorPreferences: caves and rocky overhangs mimicking limestone cave systems
subdued or no lighting (cave morph is sensitive to bright light)
smooth-edged rocks and rounded décor to prevent injury from navigation
open swimming areas — fish navigate confidently by lateral line
sparse or no planting (not naturally present in cave habitat)

diet.notes: Unfussy and eagerly accepts most aquarium foods: high-quality flake, micro-pellets, freeze-dried and frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and tubifex. Cave populations have evolved increased food-seeking behavior and enhanced olfactory sensitivity relative to surface populations — they locate food extremely efficiently in total darkness. Feed 1–2 times daily; cave morphs may overeat if food is continuously available, as their metabolism is adapted for feast-or-famine cave environments.

goodWith: larger robust tetras (Buenos Aires tetra, black skirt tetra)
barbs of similar size (tiger barbs, cherry barbs)
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (fin nipping risk)
very small fish or nano species that may be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Astyanax mexicanus (cave morph). Shows characteristic depigmented, eyeless cave form.
Wikimedia Commons image of cave morph specimen. Clear lateral-line visibility, depigmented body. Photographer: H. Zell.

summary: The Blind Cave Tetra is the cave morph of Astyanax mexicanus, famous as an evolutionary biology model for regressive evolution — eyes and pigment are lost over thousands of generations in total darkness. Compensating lateral-line hypertrophy gives superb spatial orientation; collisions are rare. Peaceful and active in groups; tolerant of a wide range of water parameters. Excellent beginner fish.
careNotes: The Blind Cave Tetra is the depigmented, eyeless cave morph of Astyanax mexicanus — genetically the same species as the surface-dwelling Mexican Tetra. Cave and surface morphs can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, making this one of the most-studied examples of regressive (degenerative) evolution in vertebrates; eye loss and loss of melanin pigment have occurred independently in multiple cave populations through a combination of neutral mutation accumulation and possible pleiotropic selection. Despite the lack of functional eyes, these fish navigate remarkably well using a hypertrophied lateral line system that detects pressure waves, and they rarely collide with tank décor. Tank collisions are more likely immediately after introduction when the fish maps a new environment; smooth-edged décor is recommended. A group of 5 or more is strongly advised — these are active, social schoolers and solitary individuals show stress and increased aggression. The cave morph is well-adapted to a wide range of water conditions (pH 6.5–8.0, gH 5–25, temperature 18–25 °C) and shows no particular sensitivity to water hardness, making it highly forgiving for beginners. Subdued lighting is preferred to mimic the cave habitat, though fish adapt to normal aquarium lighting with adequate cover. Cave-adapted metabolism means individuals will eagerly feed at any time and can consume large amounts quickly — moderate, regular feeding is preferable over free-feeding to avoid obesity and water quality issues. Not suitable with very small fish or dwarf shrimp. Fin nipping of long-finned tankmates is a known tendency, especially in understocked groups.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeding is relatively straightforward and occurs readily in a mature aquarium. A dedicated breeding tank of 40–60 L with a spawning mop or fine-leaved plants (or a mesh spawning grid) is recommended, as adults will consume eggs. Slightly raise temperature to 22–24 °C and feed live or frozen foods to condition breeders. Spawning occurs in open water; up to several hundred adhesive eggs are scattered and fall to the substrate. Parents should be removed immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours at 22 °C; fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days and accept infusoria and commercial fry foods, progressing quickly to micro-crushed flake. Cave-morph fry are born without visible eyes (eye primordia develop briefly then degenerate). Captive breeding is rated easy and has been accomplished by hobbyists worldwide.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Astyanax mexicanus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided water parameters (temperature 20–25 °C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 5–25 dH), adult size (~9 cm), native range (cave populations in San Luis Potosí/Tamaulipas, surface populations across Mexico and Texas), breeding notes (egg scatterer, easy to breed), temperament (semi-aggressive, fin nipper), diet (omnivore, unfussy), and tank recommendations.

additional.name: FishBase — Astyanax mexicanus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (max reported 12 cm TL, typical cave morph 8–10 cm), temperature range (15–25 °C), native range (Rio Grande drainage, Mexico), and habitat notes. Confirmed both cave and surface morphs as same species (Astyanax mexicanus, formerly A. fasciatus mexicanus / Anoptichthys jordani).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Blind Cave Tetra Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist-focused care guide. Confirmed beginner-level care, tank size recommendation (~75 L / 20 gal+), group size (5+), water temperature (65–80 °F / 18–27 °C), omnivore diet, fin-nipping tendency with slow/fancy fish, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Blind Cave Tetra
additional.notes: Supplemental care data and evolutionary biology context. Confirmed lateral line hypertrophy and echolocation-like spatial mapping, depigmentation, eye degeneration, and that cave and surface forms can interbreed. Noted fin-nipping tendency and preference for groups. Also confirmed easy breeding and egg-scattering reproduction.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Astyanax mexicanus Profile
additional.notes: Additional evolutionary biology context on regressive evolution (multiple independent cave population origins, genetic basis of eye loss), water parameter tolerance, cave adaptation metabolism, and historical trade notes. Confirmed longevity (5–10 years in captivity) and the historical synonym Anoptichthys jordani.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeds readily in a dedicated 40–60 L tank with spawning mop or mesh grid. Remove adults immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry free-swimming within 3–5 days. Cave-morph fry are born with brief eye primordia that quickly degenerate.
